name: Chao Tom (Shrimp and Sugar Cane Rolls)
recipeIngredient: 1 Cucumber, peeled and cut into thin slivers
12 Butter or red leaf lettuce leaves
recipeInstructions:
1. The Vietnamese use a small indoor earthen stove fueled with coal set on the table to simmer, boil and barbecue. I have broiled this dish in an oven, with excellent results. Sugar cane makes this recipe visually exciting and exotic. Its sweetness subtly melts into the shrimp paste. Sugar cane comes fresh and canned in better Asian markets; the former is scarce and very expensive. Check with the grocery clerk to make certain that you are buying 6- to 7-inch long stalks, not cubes.
2. Shell and devein the shrimp. Toss with salt; let sit for 10 minutes. Rinse with cold water; drain thoroughly. Blot dry.
3. In a food processor, finely mince the garlic and shallots. Add sugar, pepper, toasted rice powder, fish sauce, and shrimp; process into a smooth paste.
4. With the machine running, pour the ice water through the feed tube; process until the shrimp is light and fluffy. Cover and refrigerate.
5. Pour vegetable oil into a small bowl. Place a wire cooling rack on a baking sheet; brush with oil.
6. Cut the sugar cane lengthwise into quarters to make 12 long strips.
7. Dip your fingers into the oil, then take about 2 tablespoons shrimp paste and evenly mold a 1-inch cylinder around a sugar cane strip, leaving 1 inch free at both ends.
8. Arrange the rolls on the rack diagonally, keeping them from touching each other.
9. Arrange cucumber, mint, coriander leaves, and lettuce on a platter; set aside.
10. Broil the shrimp rolls about 6 inches from the heat, turning once, until edges are bright orange and filling feels firm to touch, 2 to 3 minutes per side.
11. To serve, place a rice paper sheet on a plate. Dip a pastry brush into water bowl.
12. Brush the entire rice paper generously with water. Let it sit until pliable and somewhat flimsy.
13. Put a lettuce leaf on one end of the paper.
14. Place cucumber sliver, mint, coriander leaves on top of lettuce.
15. Take a hot sugar cane stick, break off shrimp and place on vegetables.
16. Begin rolling up the paper to enclose filling; form into cylinder shape. Drip nuoc cham dipping sauce onto roll and take a bite, chewing on sugar cane for sweetness (do not swallow).
17. NUOC CHAM DIPPING SAUCE: Grind garlic, chiles, and sugar into paste in mortar, blender or mini-food processor. Stir in fish sauce, lime juice, and water. Strain into dipping bowl.
18. If sugar cane is unavailable, use a skewer or inexpensive bamboo chopsticks. Soak them in water overnight before wrapping with shrimp paste.
19. Makes 12 rolls or serves 6.
